comments.title: Family of the Gods and Planetary Aspects Question
comments.content:
Good evening!

Happy Mother's Day to those of the commentariat for whom it applies, and for everyone else, a very pleasant Sunday/Monday. To JMG, thanks as always for hosting this space.

First, something to share: for anyone interested in comparative mythology, the Family of the Gods series by William G. Davey takes a look at different Indo-European mythologies to find a shared origin, with a focus on family dynamics. The series as a whole makes an overall argument that is worthwhile, but you can get a lot out of reading The Family of the Gods and the book about whichever mythology is most interesting to you. Those with Druidical interests might be especially interested in the Arthurian and Welsh Mythology and Irish Mythology books.

Secondly, for a question: my wife has always been interested in natal astrology, though mostly just at the deeper end of the popular flavor of the subject. As such, when I started talking about the aspects of planets in our charts, she wasn't very familiar and asked me what the difference was between "positive" and "negative" aspects, especially where potentially malefic planets are involved, since it's not quite "positive=good" and "negative=bad". My best shot at an explanation is that positive aspects strengthen both planets involved and tend to bring about their better sides, and where seemingly opposed, complement each other, whereas negative aspects bring out the downsides of each planet's energies, and are more likely to hinder each other. Is that roughly right?

Thanks as always for this space and your efforts,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Family of the Gods and Planetary Aspects Question
replies.content:
1) Hmm! Interesting. I'll see if my local library system picks then up.

2) Another way to think of them is "comfortable" and "challenging." Trines and sextiles are comfortable -- the planetary energies blend smoothly and support each other. Squares and oppositions are challenging -- the planetary energies clash and it can take hard work to reconcile them in your own life.

replies.title: Re: Family of the Gods and Planetary Aspects Question
replies.content:
On 1) poking around again (I got these several years back), I'm only able to find the books on Amazon for Kindle, which is, of course, discouraging. On the plus side, they're rather cheap ($1.99 per volume). I also can't find any other material on Davey, so no credibility markers (for better or worse) other than the books themselves. If anyone is looking for a bit more to go on: these books focus largely on historical linguistic shared roots, but with a hefty dose of common mythological themes (such as "in both of these stories, there were twins, and one of the twins was killed. In the Indian version, we get such and such details about the surviving brother, but in the Greek version we have no record of that, so maybe the Indian story is suggestive"). It's the kind of stuff you'll be used to if you've looked much into comparative mythology and might find wildly speculative if you haven't. Take with the appropriate amount of salt!

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Family of the Gods and Planetary Aspects Question
replies.content: Re: "Family of the Gods" by William G. Davey.  This sounds really interesting.  I'll see about getting a copy.  I've been wondering about the relationship between the different Indo-European gods.  I have a little experience with ADF, and I know some members there are into a reconstructed proto-Indo-European polytheism.  Can you tell us much Davey's overall argument?

replies.title: Re: Family of the Gods and Planetary Aspects Question
replies.content:
Sure!

It's been a while since I read these in detail, but from a quick re-skim and what I remember, the argument is that from analyzing Indian, Persian, Greek, Norse, Arthurian/Welsh, and Irish myths, by looking at philological and story motif similarities, you can identify seven individuals who make up the core "family of the gods," distinct generations that make up that family, and several events that they were involved with (reconciliation of two different peoples, sacking of an enemy city, the creation of the world from the sacrifice and dismemberment of a being, assassination of a close relative by an arrow, and so forth). Perhaps most relevant to Druids, he argues that many elements of the Arthurian myths are best understood as echoes of earlier Proto-Indo-European myths, and therefore are less likely to reflect the historical actions of a British king fighting the Saxons (for example, the attempt to drown Mordred on Merlin's advice is paralleled by a story about someone trying to drown a young Krishna).

Personally, I found it most useful for highlighting some potential parallels to investigate that I hadn't known about, rather than the specific PIE reconstruction he's attempting. For example, he argues that the Indian story of Rama shooting Balin, the brother of Sugriva, with an arrow is a parallel with the story of Balder. Rama is an incarnation of Vishnu, who also has the name Lokayoni, which is similar to Loki, Sugriva is a version of Shiva, who is also named Uttaraka, which is similar to Hotherus, and Balin is similar to Balder. I'm not sure that I 100% buy his conclusions, especially since I've learned more since the last time I read it, but at a minimum, he provides some productive avenues to look down!

Hope this helps,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Family of the Gods and Planetary Aspects Question
replies.content: I can't help but see some vague parallels with historical Jesus studies.  Scholars examine the different canonical gospels, other New Testament writings, some apocryphal works like the Gospel of Thomas, and then try to reconstruct the so-called historical Jesus.  They often end up with a bewildering array of historical Jesii!  That said, I've enjoyed reading those studies and so I will enjoy Davey's attempt at reconstructing the PIE pantheon and mythology.

replies.title: Re: Family of the Gods and Planetary Aspects Question
replies.content: I just looked it up on Amazon in my country (Germany), and sadly, they have only volume 1, 6, and 7 available... and only as ebooks.

replies.title: Re: Family of the Gods and Planetary Aspects Question
replies.content:
Oh, weird, it's the same on the American site. I went to books 2-5 in my Kindle library and clicked through and they say "not currently available for purchase". I also can't find much of anything about the author, other than he has "Belen, New Mexico; Tucson, Arizona" under his name on the title page of the ebooks.

If it's any help, book 7 is the "tying together" of books 1-6, so if you are primarily interested in the reconstructed PIE myth(s) that he argues for, as opposed to the evidence from one of the daughter cultures, that might still get you most of the way there. I found a fairly in-depth review of the book here: https://chakra37.blogspot.com/2012/11/family-of-gods-volume-7-of-indo.html

replies.title: Re: Family of the Gods and Planetary Aspects Question
replies.content: I'm always a little leery of authors who don't reveal much about their background, at least when it comes to nonfiction genres like this.  I don't need an author who is a bona fide scholar in the subject they're writing about, but it would be nice to know what their experience and qualifications are.  There's an author who has written several good books on understanding the Gospel of Thomas and near-death experiences from a Catholic perspective, but he (or she?) lists nothing about their qualifications.  Just the initials of their first and middle name, along with their full last name. And even then, I'm not sure if that's a pen name or not.

replies.title: Re: Family of the Gods and Planetary Aspects Question
replies.content:
Yeah, I generally agree, though I realize that the closer you get to esoteric stuff, the more likely you'll be dealing with pseudonyms and the like. In looking back through these books today, I'm also struck that he gives very little in the way of citations, which is usually not a huge deal for me, but when it comes to arguments based on linguistic and/or mythological parallels, I'd like a bit more detail on how things have been reconstructed and determined to be equivalent.

That being said, when I read this a few years back, I remember it being more or less consistent with what I then knew about comparative linguistics and mythology, but I know more now, so I might have to revisit. Also, I believe the author ends up making the claim that the seven-generation family he identifies was an actual historical human family among the Proto-Indo-Europeans, which strikes me as euhemerization on steroids, and likely taking the exercise too far.

For a counter-example, Odin: the One-Eyed God by Kris Kershaw is another where it's hard to find out much about the author, but he (or possibly she! I've found references claiming both) is exquisitely scrupulous with footnotes, so if you wanted to, you could reconstruct his/her argument from the evidence yourself.

Cheers,
Jeff

comments.content:
Hi JMG & all,
I notice this month that we have a new moon on the first and last day of the month. The second new moon is sometimes called a 'Black Moon' (vs. the second Full Moon in a month being called a 'Blue Moon').
There is also a lunar eclipse in the middle of the month.

Is there any astrological or occult significance to this conjunction of Black Moon and Eclipse? I don't think it happens often, and it seems malefic to me.

How would I find out the last few times that this combination happened, and if there were events linked with its occurrences?

--Mauve Bulbous Mandrake

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Black moons and blue moons don't have any astrological significance, since they're artifacts of the civil calendar -- that is to say, the weird way we divide the year into twelve not quite equal portions. Older calendars, which are more attuned to the cosmos, begin the month on or shortly after the new moon, and so don't have this problem.

Black moons, like blue moons, are tolerably common, appearing every two or three years. To find out the last few times, find an online calendar that includes the full moons and start paging back; you should have plenty over the last decade.

replies.title: Calendars
replies.content:
Hello!

"Older calendars, which are more attuned to the cosmos, begin the month on or shortly after the new moon, and so don't have this problem."

Can we have a bit more details about why lunar calendars are more attuned to the cosmos?

Many thanks

replies.title: Re: Calendars
replies.content: Er, please think about it a little. The Moon is part of the cosmos. Its cycles affect things on earth -- check the tides, for example, or talk to any menstruating woman. Thus a calendar that actually tracks lunar cycles is more attuned to the cosmos than a calendar that doesn't.

replies.title: Re: Calendars
replies.content:
For a discussion of a mostly-lunar calendar that also makes efforts not to come unmoored from the solar year, The Sun, the Stars, and the Moon by Thorbeorht Ealdorblotere, available here: https://www.lulu.com/en/us/shop/thorbeorht-ealdorblotere/by-the-sun-the-stars-and-the-moon/paperback/product-1z7mwerw.html?page=1&pageSize=4

It's a discussion of the calendar kept by the Anglo-Saxons before they adopted the Roman calendar, so it may not be as helpful to folks not interested in Anglo-Saxon and/or Germanic heathenry, but it does a good job of talking about the different cycles of the heavenly bodies and how they can give you different ways of dividing up time, as well as how you might combine them (for example, something like "the first full moon after the Solstice"). If you find the system presented to your liking, it also tells you the dates for each adjusted lunar month in the normal civil calendar for each year through, I think, 2030 or so.

If you happen to find it *exactly* to your liking, the companion book To Keep the Holy Tides by the same author explains what holidays go with what points on the calendar, with some discussion about what we know or can infer about how the Anglo-Saxons celebrated it.

replies.title: Re: Calendars
replies.content: There are plenty of combined solar-lunar calendars; the Jewish calendar is one that's still very much in use today, and the traditional Chinese calendar is also used in some Chinese diaspora communities. I'm glad to see more work going into another of these. I've occasionally thought of putting some work into exploring the Coligny calendar, a Gaulish Celtic calendar of roughly Roman date; it's the same sort of solar-lunar arrangement.

replies.title: (no subject)

Don't apologize -- it's a reasonable question.

There are rituals in which the presence of blood is a bad idea, and most older religious traditions have specific rules to tell people when they should avoid ritual practice and how they can purify themselves. Ordinary prayer is always appropriate, no matter what the circumstances. Only if you're entering a consecrated space or handling sacred things does blood become an issue.

comments.title: Logic
comments.content:
Hey JMG-
Something that I have been pondering recently, after learning about the bizarre hyper-logic of demons and studying Discordianism, is -
What is the appropriate role of logic?
Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Logic
replies.content:
Logic is basically a user's manual for the narrow range of mental processes that we call rational thinking. It's extremely useful if you want to make sure your thinking isn't self-contradictory and confused. The problems with logic pop up when you try to apply it to things it's not suited to handle, such as interpersonal relationships, or the natural world (which is not logical in most of its workings).

So like any other tool, it's exceedingly useful for those things it's suited for, and not useful at all for the rest of existence. One of its great virtues, however, is that since it's a user's manual for human reason, nonhuman intelligences generally can't imitate it unless they're very far beyond us in intelligence. Demons are smarter than we are but their minds are oddly limited, and they can't fake our kind of reason at all.

replies.title: Re: Eldritch Old Ones?
replies.content:
The Eldritch Old Ones, aka the Great Old Ones, are the fictional deities invented by horror-fantasy author H.P. Lovecraft for his Cthulhu Mythos stories. A lot of people have found them to be surprisingly, well, real. I borrowed them for my giddy rewrite of the Cthulhu Mythos, The Weird of Hali, in which the Great Old Ones are the old gods of nature and their worshippers are the good guys, not the villains (as they were in Lovecraft's stories). Several people have taken up invoking the versions of the Great Old Ones in my novels using the SoP, and weirdly, they're getting results. If you're not familiar with the Lovecraft pantheon, this post might help:

https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/42378.html

As for the SoP, you can invoke any imaginable set of deities using it. I'd assign them to the directions this way:

East: Hastur
South: Phauz
West: Cthulhu
North: Yhoundeh
Below: Shub-Ne'hurrath
Above: Yog-Sothoth

And the opening gesture would be made to the following classic words:

"From the wells of night to the gulfs of space, and from the gulfs of space to the wells of night, ever the praises of Great Cthulhu, of Tsathoggua, and of Him Who is not to be Named. Ever Their praises, and abundance to the Black Goat of the Woods. Iä! Shub-Ne'hurrath! The Goat with a Thousand Young!"

comments.title: Sadhu and Hall
comments.content:
Morning John
Thank you for continuing to run these weekly Monday advice sessions. Recently I bought a copy of Theurgy, the art of effective worship by Mouni Sadhu which you had recommended. I’m finding it very interesting. But I found an apparent contradiction with the excellent articles by Manly P Hall. replies.title: Re: Sadhu and Hall
replies.content: Of course not. Occult teachers disagree from time to time, especially if they come to questions from different perspectives. Sadhu was a Christian, and it's central to Christian theology that you can't bear the burden of your karma/sins yourself, but Jesus can do it for you. Hall was a Platonist (and also an esoteric Buddhist), and it's central to both those traditions that you can and will deal with your karma/sins and so you might as well get busy. Which is true? Why, meditate on it and see what you think. In the meantime, the practical advice -- i.e., clean up your act, don't maltreat other people, and get busy on your spiritual life -- is shared by both sides.

replies.title: Re: Sadhu and Hall
replies.content:
Thanks John,
It seems to me these two views are extreme opposites to each other and that the truth may lie somewhere between the two. I shall set aside some time to meditate on this.
Regards Averagejoe

replies.title: Re: Sadhu and Hall
replies.content:
A dissenting opinion:

I think that Hall is using Karma in the traditional Buddhist sense, roughly equivalent to "causality" in modern English. Sadhu seems to mean it in the more popular sense of negative consequences of ones actions.

Imagine that you have just stepped in front of a car:

Using Hall's definition; you have done the stepping. There will be consequences as you are no longer where you once were. What those consequences will be are still uncertain; you might get back out of the way or the driver might stop in time. But whatever happens will unfold from you stepping out.

Using Sadhu's definition; you might have done something foolish, but it is still possible for somebody to intervene and pull you to safety. Your actions have results, but you won't necessarily be smashed just because you tried to do something that would probably and predictably lead to getting smashed.

Hopefully this is useful to you.

replies.title: Re: Sadhu and Hall

replies.title: Re: Sadhu and Hall
replies.content:
That is, of course, most true. Though as a point of irony, my disagreement was basically about whether or not they were really in disagreement.

Hopefully this doesn't sound too disagreeable or contentious.

replies.title: Re: Planetary charity
replies.content: This may sound unexpected, but Venus was the patron of the Roman sewage system -- they said prayers and made offerings to her as Venus Cloacina, "Venus of the sewers." I'd recommend a Venus charity, and simply asking for help with the situation.

replies.title: Re: Planetary charity

replies.title: Re: Planetary charity
replies.content: One minor caveat from a newbie Roman Polytheist (no degree either, so take it with a grain of salt) but coins show two figures on a dais placed over the river that would eventually become Rome's main sewer outlet, and some scholarly evidence suggests that Cloacina was a native italic goddess that fell victim to the Interpretatio Romana because she was heavily associated with Myrtles just like Venus. So Venus Cloacina may not be an epithet but the composite goddess Venus-Cloacina (which, despite seeming like a silly human idea to mash two gods together and worship the result, apparently works incredibly well). So to the Anon, consider also prayer to Venus-Cloacina, Cloacina herself, and perhaps planting some Myrtle trees.

replies.title: Re: Baptism by proxy
replies.content: No one can actually baptize you against your will. (I have severe doubts about the efficacy of infant baptism as anything but a baby blessing.) So, no, you don't have to worry about it; if someone does that, they keep the karma themselves.

replies.title: Re: Baptism by proxy
replies.content:
Thank you...that makes sense, and I thought/hoped that was the answer but I'm still pretty new to all of this. My family and I have pretty much avoided religion since leaving but of late I've been looking into what else might be out there. A friend recommended your blog and I've jsut started reading recently.

Any advice for a 30-year-old just starting to look into religious possibilities in general? I don't really have any set beliefs at this point, my parents brought me up pretty flat out agnostic but lately I've been getting more of a feeling like there might be something out there.

replies.title: Re: Baptism by proxy
replies.content:
First of all, relax and take it easy. The mainstream religions of Western cultures tend to try to panic people into thinking they have to hurry into religion as fast as possible before the monsters under the bed get them, or some equivalent; one of the teachings of the occult tradition, by contrast, is that you have as many lives as you need, so you can take some time and avoid stressing out about it.

Second, I'd encourage you to visit the public library and get a couple of beginner's books about world religions. While you're there, consider picking up a few books about the myths -- that is, sacred stories; the Bible is a collection of myths in this sense -- of various faiths and traditions. There are a lot of fascinating stories about gods and spirits out there, and reading some of them and getting a sense of what they're like is a good way to start your search.

comments.content:
This might be an obnoxious question, so I understand if it doesn't get through.

Are you familiar with anybody who's had good results from Enochian magic?

Also, was it ever a part of the Holy Golden Dawn and/or the traditions that became the FHR?

I've seen plenty of claims about how powerful it is, but I've never seen anybody claim that they actually had good or useful results come from it. My understanding was that both Dion Fortune and Paul Foster Case both denounced it, with DF completely stripping out the Enochian material she inherited from the GD in her tradition. I've also encountered the claim that it wasn't part of the original order and was later added in by Regardie's teachers.

Thanks, as always.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Yes, I have, but I've known far more people who had bad results.

2) No. It was dropped like a hot rock by Arthur Edward Waite, who was the source through whom Juliet Ashley got her GD charter and neither Ashley nor John Gilbert put it back. Generally speaking, the Holy GD and the Magickal GD didn't teach much ceremonial magic -- both Ashley and Gilbert felt that this was massively overemphasized in modern occultism, and liked to focus on meditation, scrying, and divination instead.

3) The Enochian material was part of the original GD Adept curriculum -- the GD lectures on the subject were written by Westcott and Mathers. The people I know who have had good results with it left it, as the GD curriculum instructed, until practically everything else in the Order's training program had been mastered, and then brought it in. It doesn't seem to be functional as a standalone system, but if you treat it as a supplement to the whole GD system, it can be potent stuff.

Mind you, I also left it out of the version of the GD system I included in my books Paths of Wisdom and Circles of Power, so draw your own conclusions...

comments.title: traditional african magic
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I have a couple questions about natural magic and blood sacrifice inspired by a book I’ve been reading, In Sorcery’s Shadow by Paul Stoller and Cheryl Oakes. It’s a memoir/ethnography about magic in a West African/Sahel ethnic group called the Songhay. It’s one of those rare accounts that doesn’t dismiss magic out of hand (the author is skeptical at first but comes to believe thoroughly in its efficacy over the course of a few years) and yet doesn’t turn into new age Castaneda-esque regurgitation. I’m enjoying it and I would recommend it to people interested in African magical traditions, the interplay between Islam and traditional religions, and to people just interested in comparing magical practices.

1) Half speculation half question: many of the rituals performed in this book involve (sometimes multiple) animal sacrifices. This seems like a common theme in other accounts of African magic I’ve read, and I know it happens in Santeria and some traditions of Voodoo. Also, there seems to be widespread use of harmful magic (i.e. explicitly trying to harm others) in much of Africa. Now, my understanding of your stance is that blood magic tends to attract malicious entities and harmful magic tends to accumulate negative energies around the practitioner. Might the widespread use of these types of magic therefore be a contributing factor in all the violence, instability, famine, disease, etc. that one so often hears about in Africa? Of course if everyone is getting cursed all the time that could contribute, too…

2) The magic described in this book seems to largely be a natural magic framework: mixing all sorts of plants and herbs and animal materials, combined with magical incantations and some prayers to spirits and gods. If the account is to be believed, this tradition seems like it has some very powerful/efficacious magic. In fact, as far as practical magic is concerned, it seems on par with any system of ceremonial magic I’ve heard of. I mention this because it seems like in the West, natural magic seems to have a reputation something like “magic with training wheels”. What are your thoughts on the potential power of natural magic compared to other types of magic? It occurs to me that maybe because of the simplification of many forms of Western magic (just like astrology was simplified) maybe most accessible forms of Western natural magic are like home cooking, while the unbroken African traditions may be like full blown chemistry: you can get some great things out of home cooking by mixing and combining ingredients, but chemistry can make massive explosions by (somewhat analogous) mixing and combining of ingredients.

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: traditional african magic
replies.content:
1) It's one of the awkward facts of occult history that those parts of the world that havea lot of malevolent magic and blood sacrifice are also the parts of the world that are most often ravaged by war, disease, and so on.

2) Natural magic can be extremely powerful. Most Western natural magic is folk magic, the sort of thing that ordinary untrained people can do well; in other traditions, the African occult traditions among them, natural magic is a skilled art and science, and yes, it can do anything ceremonial magic can do. There's a bit of traditional lore in occult writings that Africa, like the Tamil country of southern India, inherits a lot of its magical legacy from the Lemurian age, and so it's got a much older magical heritage than the West does. (Ours is mostly Atlantean in origin.)

replies.title: Re: traditional african magic
replies.content:
Hi JMG,

That makes a lot of sense to me.


Speaking of the Tamil, do you, or anyone else on here, know of good sources on any Indian magic?

I just remembered another Africa-related question I had. You’ve mentioned before that you think Geomancy may have developed originally from African systems of magic and divination. I wanted to look into that connected a bit more deeply. Do you know of any sources that I could look into about that?

Thanks

replies.title: Re: traditional african magic
replies.content:
1) I wish I did. Anyone else?

2) I haven't found any practical information on African divination systems other than Ifa, which is hugely complex. In general surveys, however, I've read brief accounts of a couple of dozen African divination systems that all use sequences of four binary digits, like geomantic figures. Here again, though, I don't have sources to hand. Anyone else?

replies.title: Re: traditional african magic
replies.content:
Regarding good sources on Indian magic, I have snooped around for years and found nothing. That does not mean that such books/documents do not exist; however, I can confidently say that virtually all of them will be in the local languages AND they are very strictly guarded. This even applies more broadly to occult knowledge in general such as divination (other than vedic astrology which has gone mainstream). An example to illustrate: I am friends with an Indian (who lives in India) who has picked up Western geomancy and has wanted to learn the Indian version of it (called “ramal”): he even has an uncle who has books on ramal but he has strictly forbidden my friend to read them because he has not been “initiated”. My friend ended up taking a big risk by sneaking into his uncle’s study to photograph a few pages in order to satisfy his (and my) curiosity.

To my knowledge, the practice of magic in India follows a traditional, strictly enforced apprenticeship system with a whole bunch of vows which – if broken by the initiate – can bring pretty nasty blowback on several planes. The chances of finding an authentic book on Indian magic in a European language is exceedingly small.

Ron M

replies.content: 1) This makes perfect sense in light of blowback though...

comments.title: different divination methods at the same time
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I’ve been doing the DMH material, including Ogham divination. I’m still very much a noob, but I’ve been getting surprisingly accurate responses from the ogham. I’m interested in trying other divination methods, specifically runes and geomancy. As always, thank you for this forum

replies.title: Re: different divination methods at the same time
replies.content:
It's standard in most magical schools to learn several kinds of divination. The Golden Dawn used to insist that all its members become proficient in tarot, geomancy, and astrology before they could advance to the higher grades; my teacher John Gilbert used to expect his students to show basic proficiency in seven methods of divination, and so on. That is to say, no, there's no problem.

The runes-and-ogham thing isn't a matter of danger. Lots of people in the Druid scene have found that the people who are good at Ogham aren't good at runes, and vice versa. As for other magical workings, I recommend finishing the DMH training first, but once you've worked your way through the book, by all means try other things if you like. That's a good way to expand your knowledge of magic.

comments.title: Mabinogion themes
comments.content:
Good evening,
I've recently started working through the Dolmen Arch curriculum. Discursive meditation was going well during my study of the seven cantrefs, but last Friday, I began on the first branch of the Mabinogion and now I'm stuck.
I've read versions of this tale before, and it's always disturbed me. But this one really bothered me, perhaps because it's in "undiluted" form.
I can't seem to get past the tragedy of Rhiannon's life; every time I try to choose a theme, what pops up for me is "THIS IS SOME PATRIARCHAL B.S.!" (and yes, it pops up in a shouting voice- sorry about the "all caps").
So I've thought about this, and I've decided Rhiannon's situation is partly a reflection of what a woman's life might have been in those times. It's not like she could say, "Forget this, I know I'm innocent," and go get a job and rent a tiny apartment somewhere. The tale says she tired of striving against the women who accused her and submitted to the penance.
So I meditated on the theme of different ways to deal with injustice.
Am I going about this the right way? I know there's much more to be gleaned from this tale; I just want to make sure I'm not out in left field. I'm operating on the assumption that by being receptive to Spirit Within, I'll be drawn to themes that are important for my personal growth.
Is this right?
(and thank you so much for the time and effort you put into this forum!)

replies.title: Re: Mabinogion themes
replies.content:
It's meant to be disturbing. Did you know that many scholars today believe that the Mabinogion was written by a woman? It's precisely because it reflects the realities of a woman's life in an ancient society, from a woman's perspective, that it's so troubling.

Yes, you're going about it in the right way. I'd encourage you to unpack the shouted theme a bit more, though. The fact that that's the only thing that pops into your mind, and that it pops up in an angry shout, suggests that there are things going on here unrelated to the story. Does it remind you of something in your own life, or the lives of people you know? Or is there something else hidden behind the anger? Very often, when a narrative calls up this kind of automatic anger, there's something in the story you don't want to think about.

It's worth noting, among other things, that it's not the men in the story who accuse Rhiannon, it's the other women. "Patriarchal" may not be the most accurate term for the injustice, in other words -- and of course that applies to the present as well as the past, and may relate to your life in various ways.

replies.title: Re: Mabinogion themes
replies.content:
Thanks so much for your reply...
And no, I did not know a woman most likely wrote it! That sheds some light on the tale as a whole.

And you're spot-on about the triggering nature of the story; this particular aspect has been a theme in my past. Apparently, I've still got some personal work to do...

The more I think about it, it seems that the main players were, in large part, constrained by the time in which they lived. Pwyll defended Rhiannon by refusing to put her away, and I suppose that's as much as he could do if he wanted to keep his kingdom.
The tale (at least in the first branch) doesn't mention the fate of the women who accused Rhiannon. comments.content:
When you advise against combining ceremonial magic and inebriants, does that mean do not try and mix intoxicants explicitly into one’s spiritual practices, e.g. meditating and doing ritual while under the influence, or does it go further— that if, say, pot smoking is part of one’s life at all, it’s best not to pursue occult training?

For instance, I have some less-than-ideal habits. I’m an unsustainably light sleeper and a few puffs of hash before bed corrects this like nothing else; yet for the same reason, I’m dependent on it. Nonetheless I do the sphere of protection, divination, and meditation every day, along with some simple devotional practices, and get results.

In general I have always had a difficult time going through the day consuming nothing “extra” at all, i.e. lots of coffee, nicotine, and moderate amounts of alcohol. I have gone without these things for stretches but wind up choosing to return, then feeling much better. I keep altered states separate from my daily practices and associated ideas about my own spiritual development, though, and my goal of course is one day to be at equilibrium without chemical help. For now, however, I sure appreciate it. Can you comment on my situation, and how you’d proceed?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Magical training doesn't require complete abstention from psychoactive compounds; most ceremonial magicians use some caffeinated beverage or other -- I guzzle improbable amounts of green tea -- and modest use of alcohol, tobacco, etc. don't seem to be a problem.  Caffeine doesn't interfere with practices unless you take it in excess -- Zen monks are notorious tea drinkers. The others? Don't do your practices while under the influence. As for outgrowing the use of such things, "outgrowing" is the crucial point; you'll know you're ready to let go of them when you no longer feel attracted to them.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: This is not medical advice. But in my experience, those who have trouble sleeping often have emotional traumas - you're conscious brain doesn't want to shut off and leave the subconscious brain and it's storehouse of trauma in charge. Again, not advice, but in my experience spending some time dealing with your traumas head on can help sleep considerably. I've known many to get off sleeping tablets this way. It can take quite a while though, if the trauma is buried deep down.
